As a shadow, this points to Christ’s Spirit-directed mission: ‘the Spirit of the Lord is upon me, because he has anointed me to proclaim good news’ (Luke 4:18). What the Spirit specified for the temple-builder, the Spirit enacts through the Messianic builder of the eternal temple.
28.13: The plan included the divisions of priests and Levites, all the service work, and all the vessels of service. The Spirit-given plan extends from architecture to personnel to equipment — comprehensive detail for every element of worship. As a shadow, Christ’s comprehensive provision for the church similarly covers personnel (gifts of apostles, prophets, teachers, Eph 4:11), practice (the Lord’s Supper, baptism, prayer), and equipping (the Spirit’s gifts, 1 Cor 12:7-11). The Solomonic temple’s total specification is the type of the church’s total equipping.
28.14: The weights of gold and silver for all the articles: precisely specified by David from the Spirit-received plan. The attention to material weight reflects the seriousness with which sacred objects were treated — not approximate but exact. As a revelation of God: the divine architect specifies material quantities with precision. The NT equivalent is the precision of Christ’s atoning work — not approximate reconciliation but exact, sufficient, and complete: ‘he has appeared once for all at the end of the ages to put away sin by the sacrifice of himself’ (Heb 9:26). Nothing imprecise about the atonement.
28.15: The exact weight for each gold and silver lampstand and its lamps. The lampstands in the temple provided light for the holy place. As a type, the gold lampstand (menorah) represents the light of the divine presence — ‘your word is a lamp for my feet’ (Ps 119:105); ‘I am the light of the world’ (John 8:12). Revelation 1:12-13 depicts the risen Christ standing among seven golden lampstands. The Solomonic lampstands are the type; Christ as the light of the world who walks among his churches is the fulfillment.
28.16: The weight of gold for each table of the bread of the Presence (leḥem happānîm, ‘bread of the face/presence’). As a type, the bread of the Presence — twelve loaves set before YHWH each Sabbath (Lev 24:5-9) — directly anticipates Christ: ‘I am the bread of life; whoever comes to me shall not hunger’ (John 6:35). The bread set before the face of God is the shadow of the one who, as both God and man, is simultaneously the one who presents and the one presented. Christ is the bread of the Presence who gives himself to be eaten.
28.17: The pure gold forks, basins, and cups; the golden bowls and silver bowls — all specified by weight. The service vessels that handled the blood of sacrifice and the grain of offering were all precisely specified. As a shadow, each sacrificial vessel points to the instruments of Christ’s priestly work. Hebrews 9:21-22 notes that ‘under the law almost everything is purified with blood’ — the vessels of the Solomonic temple were the hardware of the sacrificial system whose every element Christ fulfilled in his single self-offering (Heb 9:26-28).
28.18: Refined gold for the incense altar; and the plan for the golden chariot — the cherubim who spread their wings over the ark of the covenant. The chariot of the cherubim (rekeb hakkĕrûḇîm) is the throne-chariot of YHWH (Ezek 1:4-28; 10:1-22). As a type, the cherubim overshadowing the ark image the divine throne-room in heaven. Hebrews 9:5 mentions ‘the cherubim of glory overshadowing the mercy seat’ — into whose presence Christ has now entered with his own blood (Heb 9:12). The golden chariot plan is the shadow of the heavenly throne before which Christ intercedes.
28.19: ‘All this came to me in writing from the LORD’s hand — every detail of the plan.’ The divine origin of the temple blueprint is asserted in the strongest possible terms — written by YHWH’s hand, not devised by David. As a revelation of God, this parallels Moses receiving the law written by God’s finger (Exod 31:18) and Christ speaking only what the Father gave him to speak (John 12:49: ‘the Father who sent me has himself given me a commandment — what to say and what to speak’). The divine origin of the plan guarantees its sufficiency and authority.
28.20: ‘Be strong and courageous and do it. Do not be afraid or dismayed, for the LORD God, even my God, is with you.’ David’s charge to Solomon draws on the language of Moses to Joshua (Deut 31:7-8; Josh 1:6, 9). This same promise is applied to all believers in Christ: ‘he has said, “I will never leave you nor forsake you.” So we can confidently say, “The Lord is my helper’’ (Heb 13:5-6). The succession formula — be strong, do not fear, God is with you — reaches its permanent fulfillment in Christ as Emmanuel (‘God with us’, Matt 1:23) who promised: ‘I am with you always, to the end of the age’ (Matt 28:20).
28.21: ‘The priestly and Levitical divisions for all the service of God’s house are ready.’ The work of preparation is complete; the appointed servants are in place; the skilled workers are available. Everything is ready for the temple-builder. As a revelation of God, this completeness of preparation reflects the ‘fullness of time’ principle: ‘when the fullness of time had come, God sent forth his Son’ (Gal 4:4). Christ came when the Law, the prophets, the priesthood, and the covenant institutions had been fully established. He did not build the eternal temple in a vacuum but into a world where all the preparations had been completed.
